Name: information_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of model compression can be characterized using rate-distortion theory, which provides a framework for understanding the trade-off between model complexity and accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored various aspects of model compression, including mixed-precision quantization, post-training quantization, and adaptive mixed-precision training. However, these works have not explicitly considered the information-theoretic limits of model compression. Our proposal builds upon these works by providing a rate-distortion framework for understanding the fundamental limits of model compression.
Abstract: Model compression is a crucial technique for reducing the computational and memory requirements of deep neural networks. However, the fundamental limits of model compression are not well understood. In this proposal, we aim to characterize the information-theoretic limits of model compression using rate-distortion theory. We will develop a rate-distortion framework that provides a trade-off between model complexity and accuracy, and use this framework to derive achievable bounds on the compression ratio of deep neural networks. Our approach will provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the limits of model compression and will have implications for the design of efficient neural network architectures.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to validate our rate-distortion framework and evaluate the achievable bounds on the compression ratio of deep neural networks. We will use benchmark datasets such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10, and evaluate the performance of our approach using metrics such as accuracy, FLOPS, and memory usage. We will also compare our approach with state-of-the-art model compression techniques to demonstrate its effectiveness.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our rate-distortion framework may not capture all the complexities of model compression, and may not provide a complete characterization of the fundamental limits of model compression. Another limitation is that our approach may require significant computational resources to derive the achievable bounds on the compression ratio of deep neural networks.
